title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: The Hidden Kingdom: The Power of Mycelium
content.text: When we walk through a forest, we see trees, flowers, and the occasional mushroom. But beneath our feet lies a vast, complex network that is responsible for the health of the entire ecosystem. This is the mycelium, a web of thread-like fungal cells that can stretch for miles. || (41) || [41] Often called 'nature's internet,' this network allows plants to communicate and share resources [/41]. It is a biological infrastructure that has existed for millions of years, yet we are only just beginning to understand its potential to solve modern human problems. || (42) || [42] One of the most exciting applications is in the field of 'myco-fabrication' [/42]. Companies are now using mycelium to grow sustainable alternatives to plastic, leather, and even building materials. By feeding the fungi agricultural waste, researchers can create solid objects in a matter of days. || (43) || [43] This process is carbon-negative and produces a material that is fully biodegradable [/43]. Unlike traditional plastics, which last for centuries, a mycelium-based package can be broken down in a garden compost heap in a few weeks. || (44) || [44] Beyond packaging, mycelium is also being used in 'mycoremediation' [/44]. This is the process of using fungi to clean up polluted environments. Fungi are natural decomposers; they produce powerful enzymes capable of breaking down complex toxins, including petroleum and certain types of radioactive waste. || (45) || [45] In one experiment, oil-soaked soil was treated with oyster mushroom mycelium [/46]. Within weeks, the oil had disappeared, and the soil was healthy enough to support plant life again. This suggests that fungi could be a key tool in repairing the damage caused by industrial accidents. || (46) || [46] However, the use of fungi is not without its risks [/46]. Introducing a specific species of fungus into a new environment could have unintended consequences for the local ecosystem. We must ensure that our 'fungal solutions' do not become new ecological problems.

paragraphs.A: [42] These fungal threads act as a sophisticated chemical exchange system [/42]. They take sugars from the trees and, in return, provide them with essential minerals like phosphorus, creating a perfect symbiotic balance.
paragraphs.B: [41] Mycelium is essentially a living web of microscopic fibers [/41]. It can be found in almost every inch of soil on Earth, acting as the 'connective tissue' of the planet.
paragraphs.C: [44] This adaptability makes it an ideal candidate for a circular economy [/44]. The materials are not only grown from waste but also return to the earth as nutrients at the end of their life cycle.
paragraphs.D: [43] The resulting material is surprisingly strong and fire-resistant [/43]. Because the mycelium acts as a natural glue, it binds the waste particles together into a dense, durable block that can be molded into any shape.
paragraphs.E: [45] Fungi are unique in their ability to 'eat' things that would kill most other organisms [/45]. They don't just move the pollution; they actually dismantle the chemical bonds of the toxins, turning them into harmless organic matter.
paragraphs.F: [46] This is why many scientists are calling for a more cautious, 'ecosystem-first' approach [/46]. Before we deploy these 'super-fungi' on a large scale, we need more long-term studies to understand how they interact with native bacteria and insects.
paragraphs.G: Mycelium is also being explored as a source of food. Some species of fungi can produce high-protein meat alternatives that have a fraction of the environmental impact of traditional livestock.

questions.explanation: B define qué es el micelio físicamente (fibras microscópicas), lo cual encaja con la introducción del texto principal.
A explica el sistema de intercambio químico entre árboles y hongos, lo cual detalla el concepto de 'nature's internet'.
D describe las propiedades físicas del material resultante del proceso de 'myco-fabrication' (fuerte y resistente al fuego).
C conecta la biodegradabilidad del hongo con el concepto de 'economía circular'.
E explica la capacidad de los hongos para 'desmantelar' toxinas, lo cual precede al ejemplo del experimento con petróleo.
F conecta el riesgo de las 'consecuencias inesperadas' con la necesidad de un enfoque de 'primero el ecosistema'.
